title: Quadratic Funding and the Problem of Public Bads
body_html:
Quadratic funding is a mechanism designed to solve the public goods problem: how do you fund things that benefit everyone but that markets chronically underproduce? The mechanism is elegant — matching contributions in proportion to the square root of the number of contributors rather than the dollar amount, which heavily weights breadth of support over depth. A hundred people each donating $1 gets more matching than one person donating $100. This aligns funding with genuine community interest rather than wealth concentration.

But in experimenting with Gitcoin Grants, Vitalik Buterin discovered a structural gap in the design: quadratic funding handles public goods elegantly, but it has no mechanism for public bads. In the fourth Gitcoin round, some of the top recipients were Twitter influencers whose contributions were viewed positively by some and negatively by others. A purely neutral quadratic funding system would fund both sides of a war — it signals "this is a public good" but offers no way to signal "this is a public harm."

Buterin's proposed fix for round five was negative contributions: the ability to pay $1 to reduce a project's funding (redistributing it to others). The reaction was swift and negative — the internet memed it mercilessly, and the community largely rejected it. This failure is instructive. It reveals something deep about human psychology: we are much more comfortable coordinating around positive actions (supporting things) than negative actions (opposing things). Loss aversion and the fear of mob justice make negative coordination mechanisms feel fundamentally more dangerous than positive ones.

This asymmetry has profound implications for decentralized governance. There is a reason, Buterin notes, that the go-to example of decentralized governance going wrong is mob justice. When communities try to coordinate against something — a person, a project, a behavior — the dynamics of scapegoating, information cascades, and tribalism become much more dangerous than when communities coordinate for something. This is why even organizations with strong democratic values often leave punitive decisions to centralized bodies: a single judge applying rules is often more just than a crowd applying feelings.

The d/acc framework grapples seriously with this asymmetry. Defense-favoring governance mechanisms need to handle both sides of the ledger — not just enabling good things, but preventing or penalizing harmful ones. The challenge is doing this without creating infrastructure that can be captured and used as a censorship weapon. Community Notes works partly because its algorithm explicitly requires cross-partisan agreement, making it structurally resistant to coordinated attacks from any single faction. Prediction markets work because they require skin in the game, discouraging bad-faith participation. These design choices aren't incidental — they're attempts to build negative coordination mechanisms that are robust to capture.

The broader lesson for web3 governance is that the hard problem isn't funding public goods — quadratic funding handles that reasonably well. The hard problem is building collective decision-making mechanisms around harms, penalties, and exclusions that don't degenerate into mob rule or get captured by the most motivated minority. That's a game theory, mechanism design, and social psychology problem as much as a technical one, and it remains largely unsolved.
